b: Every frontier model is, at the bottom, a pile of hand-tuned kernels. Here is why that layer is the one worth learning.

t: The three regimes: compute, memory, overhead
b: The single most useful mental model in performance: every kernel is bound by compute, memory bandwidth, or overhead. Learn to tell which.
Arithmetic intensity, peak FLOPs vs peak bytes, and the roofline that tells you the fastest your kernel could ever be.
What hiring managers actually ask for — matmul-from-scratch to CUTLASS to AI-generated kernels — and where this site teaches each.
The two surfaces, the worklog style, the async track, and the order to read things in.
The unit of GPU compute. Sub-partitions, schedulers, and the on-chip memory that makes or breaks a kernel.
The scalar ALUs, what they can and can't do, and why they are no longer where the FLOPs are.
The matrix-multiply-accumulate engines that hold ~95% of an H100's FLOPs. Shapes, precisions, and why kernels are written around them.
How an SM hides 400-cycle memory latency by juggling dozens of warps — the reason occupancy matters.
The fastest and most contended memory on the chip. Register pressure, spills, and why 255 is a magic number.
The programmable scratchpad that co-lives with L1. 228 KiB, 32 banks, and the single most important optimization surface.
60 MiB split across two partitions with a crossbar, data compression, residency control, and near/far latency.
Stacked DRAM, through-silicon vias, the interposer, 3.35 TB/s — and why every kernel is ultimately a memory-traffic problem.
How 132 SMs are grouped, why defective SMs get fused off, and what thread-block clusters buy you.
TMA, wgmma, DSMEM, FP8, then tcgen05, TMEM and NVFP4 — the architectural deltas that each spawned new kernels.
Plotting your kernel on the roofline, reading the ridge point, and knowing when to stop optimizing.
Registers × threads × shared memory → how many warps fit, and why more occupancy is not always faster.
FLOPs per byte: the number that decides your regime and the target of every tiling trick.
How a warp's 32 loads become one 128-byte transaction — or thirty-two — and what that does to your bandwidth.
The execution hierarchy and how it maps onto SMs, plus the indexing arithmetic you will write a thousand times.
Why 32 threads share a program counter, what happens at an if-statement, and how divergence quietly halves your throughput.
Grid/block dims, launch overhead, streams, and what actually happens between <<<>>> and the first instruction.
Global, shared, local, constant, register, texture — the full map, with latencies and when to reach for each.

t: PTX vs SASS: the compilation story
b: nvcc → PTX → ptxas → SASS. What is virtual, what is real, and why you read SASS to find the truth.
sm_90a and friends — how to compile for the right architecture and why the 'a' matters on Hopper.
32 banks, the conflict rule, and the padding/swizzling fixes that recover the bandwidth you paid for.
Warp-shuffle reductions, atomicAdd, and how to sum a million numbers without serializing your GPU.
Overlapping copy and compute, cp.async, and the concurrency that keeps the SMs fed.

t: Your first kernel, end to end
b: SAXPY to RGB→grayscale: launch config, boundary checks, and benchmarking done right, from scratch.
Solving the first set of Sasha Rush's GPU Puzzles — map, zip, broadcast, and the mental model of one-thread-one-element.
Pooling, dot product, convolution and prefix sum — where shared memory and cooperation enter the picture.
One thread per output element. The baseline, the profile, and why it leaves 98% of the GPU on the table.
A one-line remap of thread indices that turns 32 scattered loads into one — a 6× win for free.
Blocking over K, staging tiles in SMEM, and cutting global-memory traffic by the tile width.
One thread computes many outputs. Register caching pushes arithmetic intensity past the memory wall.
A 2D grid of results per thread. Input reuse in registers doubles us again.
float4 / LDS.128 loads. The SASS moment where 8 instructions collapse into 2.
BM, BN, BK, TM, TN — searching the parameter space instead of guessing it.
Making every level of parallelism explicit — block, warp, thread — to reach cuBLAS territory.
Overlapping the next tile's load with this tile's math to hide the memory latency entirely.
The black box we've been chasing: heuristics, split-K, and why the last 6% is so hard.
Warmup, clocks, L2 flushing, and reporting TFLOP/s honestly — the discipline that makes the whole ladder trustworthy.
All ten kernels on one roofline, the speedups stacked, and the general principles that transfer to any kernel.

t: Tensor cores I: the WMMA GEMM
b: Rebuilding matmul on tensor cores from scratch (after alexarmbr): fragments, the mma shape, and a first working kernel.
Register-fragment layouts, SMEM swizzling to kill bank conflicts on the tensor-core path, and the profile that proves it.

t: Tensor cores III: to cuBLAS speed
b: mma.sync, double-buffered pipelines and the precision menu — a tensor-core GEMM that rivals the library.

t: Prefill vs decode: two different machines
b: Why the compute-bound prefill (GEMM) and the memory-bound decode (GEMV) demand completely different kernels.
The highest-leverage inference optimization: stop round-tripping tensors through HBM between elementwise ops.
The numerically-stable, single-pass, online softmax — the trick that makes FlashAttention possible.
Reduction + normalize + affine, fused into one pass — the workhorse normalization of modern LLMs.
QKᵀ → softmax → V as three kernels, its O(N²) HBM traffic, and why that is the problem to solve.
Fusing the whole attention into one kernel with online softmax so the N×N scores never touch HBM.
Rebalancing work across warps and the sequence dimension for a ~2× step over FA1.
Warp specialization, TMA and FP8 on Hopper — attention at the hardware's speed of light.
Why decode is memory-bound, how paging fixes fragmentation, and the kernel that reads a paged cache.
Dequantize-in-the-kernel, scale handling, and the low-precision matmuls that halve or quarter memory traffic.
Gate × up × SiLU fused — a small kernel that shows up in every transformer MLP and every kernel benchmark.
Serving many sequences one token at a time: skinny matmuls, memory-bound reality, and how batching helps.
The Tensor Memory Accelerator: hardware-managed multidimensional copies that free the threads to compute.
Warpgroup matmul and the producer/consumer pattern that keeps the tensor cores saturated on Hopper.
A full worklog (after hamzaelshafie & cudaforfun) that assembles TMA + wgmma + warp specialization into a library-beating GEMM.
The 5th-gen tensor cores, the new Tensor Memory (TMEM), and CTA pairs — a different machine to write for.
e2m1 with FP8 block scales, and the hackathon journey from a 2000µs to a 22µs FP4 GEMV.
MLA decode, FP8 MoE GEMMs and the DeepGEMM backend — production kernels behind a frontier model.

t: DSpark: speculative decoding as a kernel problem
b: V4-Pro's speculative-decoding module — draft passes, parallel verification, acceptance kernels, and CSA/HCA sparse attention.
From a naive GEMM to a real CUTLASS kernel (after kapilsh) — reading library-grade warptiling with our own vocabulary.
Layouts and tensors in CuTe, Triton in 40 lines, TileLang — the abstractions above raw CUDA and when to use them.
compute-sanitizer, user-triggered core dumps, cuda-gdb, -lineinfo and nvdisasm — finding the illegal access at 3am.
The benchmark, the fast_p metric, and why 'correct and faster than PyTorch' is a surprisingly hard bar.
Sampling 100 kernels, iterative feedback, and evolutionary/tree search — how DeepSeek-V3 went 4% → 72%.
Natural-language optimization ideas + parallel branching: LayerNorm at 484% of PyTorch — and FlashAttention at 9%.
Multi-turn RL with verifiable rewards, the KernelBook dataset, and why the human+AI+profiler loop is the real answer.
The seven ingredients in every chapter, how to read it (learn first, then teach), and how to prep a session from it.

t: The mentor's mindset: teaching hard things simply
b: You only truly understand something when you can teach it to a beginner. How to build from zero, use metaphors, and turn abstraction into arithmetic on a whiteboard.

t: The whole arc: one story, fourteen sessions
b: The single narrative — 'make the matmul fast, then make the model fast' — that ties every lecture and workshop together, drawn as one map.

t: Matrix multiplication, from the absolute beginning
sec: Mentor Handbook · Foundations You Must Feel
b: Dot products, the shape dance, a 2×2 by hand, then why C = A·B is three nested loops. The one operation everything else optimizes.

t: Why matrix multiply is the whole game
sec: Mentor Handbook · Foundations You Must Feel
b: Every linear layer, attention score, and MLP is a matmul. Trace a transformer and count the matmuls. This is why kernels matter.

t: What 'fast' actually means: time, FLOPs, bytes
sec: Mentor Handbook · Foundations You Must Feel
b: Operations, floating-point ops per second, bytes moved, and why a stopwatch is not enough. The units a mentor must own.

t: Compute vs memory: the kitchen that governs everything
sec: Mentor Handbook · Foundations You Must Feel
b: The three regimes (compute / memory / overhead) told as a kitchen story — cooking vs fetching ingredients. The master mental model.

sec: Mentor Handbook · Foundations You Must Feel
b: 989 TFLOP/s, 3.35 TB/s, 132 SMs, 228 KiB, 32 threads. The handful of H100 numbers that turn hand-waving into intuition.

sec: Mentor Handbook · Foundations You Must Feel
b: The one plot that says the fastest your kernel could ever go — a ceiling of compute and a ramp of memory — explained without intimidation.

t: CPU vs GPU: the chef and the cafeteria
b: A few brilliant chefs versus a thousand line cooks. Why GPUs trade cleverness for overwhelming parallelism, and when that wins.

t: Threads, warps & blocks: the marching band
b: One thread = one musician; a warp of 32 marches in lockstep; blocks share a room. The execution hierarchy as a parade.

t: The memory hierarchy: your desk, drawers, shelves, warehouse
b: Registers, shared memory, L2, HBM as distances you walk for a tool. Why the whole craft is keeping data close.

t: Tensor cores: the matmul machine inside the machine
b: A special unit that eats little matrices whole. Where 95% of the FLOPs live and why modern kernels are built around it.

t: Latency hiding: the short-order cook juggling tickets
b: Memory is slow, so the GPU keeps dozens of jobs in flight and always works on a ready one. Why 'occupancy' matters.

t: Coalescing: everybody boards the same bus
b: When 32 threads read neighbouring addresses, the hardware fetches them in one trip. Scattered reads waste the bus. The cheapest big win.

t: Teaching Kernel 1: the honest, slow start
sec: Mentor Handbook · Teaching the GEMM Ladder
b: One thread per output. How to run it, show it hitting 1.3% of cuBLAS, and make students hungry to fix it.

sec: Mentor Handbook · Teaching the GEMM Ladder
b: Remap two indices, 6× faster. How to build the suspense and the reveal, and draw the coalesced vs scattered bus.

sec: Mentor Handbook · Teaching the GEMM Ladder
b: Stage tiles in shared memory so the block reuses them. The reuse idea, drawn, and why we block over K.

t: Teaching Kernels 4–5: pockets full of work
sec: Mentor Handbook · Teaching the GEMM Ladder
b: Each thread computes many outputs from values held in registers. 1D then 2D tiling, and the arithmetic-intensity climb to 68.7%.

t: Teaching Kernel 6: carry four boxes at once
sec: Mentor Handbook · Teaching the GEMM Ladder
b: float4 loads and the SASS 'eight loads become two' moment — the most satisfying reveal in the course.

sec: Mentor Handbook · Teaching the GEMM Ladder
b: Autotuning and warptiling — making block, warp, and thread explicit to reach 94% of cuBLAS. How to land the finale.

sec: Mentor Handbook · Teaching the GEMM Ladder
b: Do the ladder again on tensor cores: fragments, swizzling, mma.sync. How to teach WMMA without drowning students.

t: Teaching fusion: stop driving to the warehouse
b: Why round-tripping tensors through HBM between tiny ops is the waste that fusion removes. The single biggest inference win.
Stable softmax by hand, then the running-max/running-sum trick that makes FlashAttention possible. Teach it as a running average.
QKᵀ → softmax → V, and why the giant N×N scores are the problem. Set up FlashAttention as the fix.

t: Teaching FlashAttention: never write the scores down
b: Streaming attention in tiles so the N×N matrix never touches memory. The metaphor, the tiling, and the production stakes.

t: Teaching the KV cache: why chat is memory-bound
b: Prefill vs decode, the KV cache, and why generating each token re-reads a growing memory. PagedAttention as the fix.

t: Teaching Hopper: the async delivery truck (TMA & WGMMA)
b: How the H100 added a hardware copy engine and warpgroup matmul, in words a student can hold. Why FlashAttention-3 needs it.

t: Teaching Blackwell & NVFP4: four-bit numbers that work
b: Tensor memory, and micro-scaled 4-bit formats. The 2000µs→22µs hackathon story as your hook.

t: Teaching DeepSeek & DSpark: kernels behind a frontier model
b: FlashMLA, DeepGEMM, and DSpark = speculative decoding. How an open lab ships its own kernels — the ultimate production link.
KernelBench, the 'monkeys' that sample 100 kernels, and the honest wins-and-failures. The human+AI+profiler loop — and the tie to harnesses.

t: Teaching debugging: war stories that stick
b: The race, the misaligned load, the silent NaN. compute-sanitizer, core dumps and cuda-gdb — taught as detective stories.

t: Lecture plans: L1–L4, minute by minute
b: Timings, board sequence, the one demo, and the checkpoint questions for the first four lectures.

t: Lecture plans: L5–L8, minute by minute
b: The GEMM finale, tensor cores, profiling/debugging, and attention — paced for a three-hour room.
FlashAttention, Hopper, the abstraction ladder, serving kernels, Blackwell/NVFP4, and the DeepSeek+AI finale — how to run each hands-on.

t: The questions students always ask (with crisp answers)
b: A ready bank of the recurring questions and the tight, correct answers so you're never caught flat.

t: Running the 'You vs the machine' capstone
b: How to set up, mentor, and grade the by-hand-plus-LLM kernel capstone on the worklog, not the raw speedup.
